>_ Unix · File I/O Contents Cheatsheet
📁 A BookBank field guide

Unix File I/O

How a program reads and writes the world through a handful of dead-simple verbs — open, read, write, close — and the beautiful trick that makes files, keyboards, pipes, and network sockets all look exactly the same.

in the voice of Richard Feynman · first-principles, playful, intuition-first

▶ Start reading ⚡ Cheatsheet

Before you turn the page

Here is a small miracle you use ten thousand times a day without noticing. You type into a terminal, and the same four function calls that save a photograph to disk are quietly carrying your keystrokes. A program prints its results, and whether that goes to your screen, into a file, or down a pipe to another program is a decision made outside the program entirely — and the program never knows the difference.

That is not an accident. It's the single design decision at the heart of Unix, made in a Bell Labs office in 1969 and still running the planet: make everything look like a file. A file is just a stream of bytes you can open, read from, write to, and close. Decide that keyboards and pipes and network sockets are also "just streams of bytes," give them the same four verbs, and suddenly a tool that knows how to read a file automatically knows how to read anything.

🧭
How to read this

Chapters 1–2 hand you the two ideas everything hangs on — everything is a file, and the little numbered ticket called a file descriptor. Chapters 3–6 are the four system calls in your hands: open, read/write, the three tables behind the scenes, and lseek. Chapter 7 explains the printf-versus-write mystery — buffering. Chapter 8 is the payoff: how the shell builds redirection and pipelines out of exactly these pieces.

cover ·

Table of Contents

  1. Everything Is a File The one idea. Four verbs over a stream of bytes — for files, terminals, pipes, and sockets alike. the idea
  2. The File Descriptor A small integer — a cloakroom ticket. Why 0, 1, and 2 are always stdin, stdout, stderr. the handle
  3. open(): Getting a Handle Turning a path into a descriptor. Flags that aren't bitmasks, permission modes, umask, and errno. syscall
  4. read() & write(): Moving Bytes The offset that advances itself, why a short read is normal, EOF, and the write loop you must get right. syscall
  5. The Three Tables Descriptor → open file description → inode. The picture that finally explains fork, dup, and a shared offset. the model
  6. lseek() & the Offset Random access, seeking past the end, and the strange magic of files with holes in them. position
  7. Buffered vs Unbuffered Why printf can vanish in a crash but write can't. stdio's buffer, sitting on top of the syscalls. stdio
  8. dup2, Pipes & Redirection The payoff: how the shell builds > file and a | b out of nothing but descriptors. the payoff
cover ·
🔑
About this book

Grounded in the POSIX / Unix system-call interface as documented in the Linux man-pages (open(2), read(2), write(2), lseek(2), dup(2)) and W. Richard Stevens' Advanced Programming in the UNIX Environment, and cross-checked against the University of Chicago systems-lab file-I/O notes. The C examples are POSIX and compile with any C compiler on Linux or macOS. Everything renders offline.

💡
Why learn the raw layer?

Most days you'll use a language's tidy file object, not open() directly. But every one of those objects is a thin coat of paint over these exact system calls. When a file "won't flush," when a pipe deadlocks, when a log line vanishes in a crash, when two processes clobber each other's writes — the explanation is always down here. Learn this floor and the whole building stops being mysterious.

Press ⌘R or reopen the book to refresh after edits.

⌂ Library
